scope.description: Collapse the three cartograph companion books into a single writ.ext['cartograph'] sub-slot. Includes: removing the `cartograph/{visions,charges,pieces}` Stacks books from `supportKit.books`; rewriting the six createX/transitionX paths to write writ.ext['cartograph'] inline (atomic with the writ row); rewriting the three showX/listX/patchX paths to project a {Vision,Charge,Piece}Doc shape over the writ row; updating cartograph.ts/types.ts/render.ts module docstrings and the cartograph README to match; updating cartograph.test.ts / tools.test.ts / tools/vision-apply.test.ts to drop the companion-book ensureBook calls and rewire the CDC-event tests against the writs book.
scope.rationale: The brief's central claim — non-negotiable §1, §2, §3, §4, §5. The typed-API surface stays stable; the storage shape moves to the sanctioned ext slot.

scope.description: Rewrite the surveying-cascade architecture doc to match the post-cleanup shape: §3.4 (SurveyDoc → status['surveyor']/ext['surveyor'] sub-slots; drop targetNodeId/rigName/completedAt as redundant), §3.6 (substrate watches a single writs-book CDC stream filtered by writ.type rather than three per-book streams), §3.7 (substrate plugin shape — drop books.surveys, replace 'Stamps SurveyDoc on completion' with 'Stamps status['surveyor'] on completion'). Plus incidental references elsewhere in the doc that mention `book.cartograph.{visions,charges,pieces}` get touched up for consistency.
scope.rationale: The brief's non-negotiable §6. The substrate is designed against the post-cleanup shape so Commission C is briefed against it (and so the substrate's CDC observer is one filtered subscription, not three per-book subscriptions).

decisions.question: How do createX / transitionX achieve atomic writ + ext['cartograph'] writes — direct tx.book writes (existing pattern, ext populated inline) or clerk.post / clerk.transition + clerk.setWritExt under one stacks.transaction (brief literal reading)?
decisions.context: Today's createX/transitionX bypass clerk.post and clerk.transition entirely — they open a stacks.transaction and write tx.book<WritDoc>('clerk', 'writs') directly, duplicating clerk's parent/codex/lifecycle validation byte-for-byte (cartograph.ts lines 304-307: 'the cost of being a typed atomic surface'). The brief §3 says 'wrap the clerk.transition and clerk.setWritExt calls in a single stacks.transaction'. Either pattern works — clerk.setWritExt opens its own transaction that flattens (stacks-core.ts line 192). The difference is downstream CDC: direct-tx-write emits one Phase 1 event per logical operation; clerk.transition + clerk.setWritExt emits two Phase 1 events that coalesce only at Phase 2.

options.direct-tx-writes: Keep the existing direct tx.book<WritDoc>('clerk', 'writs').put / .patch pattern. createX puts a writ with `ext: { cartograph: { stage } }` populated inline; transitionX patches phase + ext together (read existing ext, merge in cartograph sub-slot, write back). Continues to duplicate clerk.post/transition validation, but emits one Phase 1 CDC event per logical operation. Removes the two companion-book put/patch calls and that's it.
options.clerk-call-pattern: Refactor createX to clerk.post + clerk.setWritExt under stacks.transaction; refactor transitionX to clerk.transition + clerk.setWritExt under stacks.transaction. Removes the duplicated parent-validation in validateParent(...) and the duplicated lifecycle-validation in transitionX. Emits two Phase 1 CDC events per logical operation (coalesce to one at Phase 2). Matches the brief's literal wording.
options.hybrid: createX uses direct-tx-write (with ext populated inline on the put — single CDC event) because clerk.post emits its own create event and we'd add a separate update for ext. transitionX uses clerk-call-pattern (clerk.transition + clerk.setWritExt under tx) because the lifecycle validation is the heaviest duplication and is worth removing — and Phase 1 observers don't care for cartograph types (no childrenBehavior cascade engagement; phase-unchanged setWritExt update is a silent no-op for the cascade engine).

decisions.rationale: Keeps single-Phase-1-CDC-event for createX (matches the spirit of the brief's single-event-per-apply guarantee even for any future Phase 1 observer); removes the duplicated lifecycle-validation in transitionX (the heaviest duplication) by delegating to clerk.transition + clerk.setWritExt under one transaction. The Phase 1 update-then-update sequence on transition is a non-issue: clerk's children-behavior-engine is the only Phase 1 writs-book observer and (a) it skips phase-unchanged updates, (b) cartograph types declare no childrenBehavior so even phase-changes don't engage it. Phase 2 observers (sentinel, clockworks lifecycle observer) see the coalesced event regardless.

patron.rationale: No principle speaks — confirming the primer.

decisions.question: Where does codex live on a cartograph writ post-cleanup — on writ.codex (top-level WritDoc field, where it already lives today) or in writ.ext['cartograph'].codex (brief literal reading)?
decisions.context: The writ row already carries a top-level codex field (WritDoc.codex) — used by clerk's `--codex` filter, by the writs page, and by the codex-inheritance rule that propagates a parent's codex to a child on createCharge/createPiece. Today's cartograph code duplicates codex into the companion doc (writ.codex AND visionDoc.codex carry the same value). The brief §1 lists `interface CartographExt { stage; codex?: string; }` and §4 says 'project writ.ext['cartograph'].stage and writ.ext['cartograph'].codex onto the returned shape' — a literal reading puts codex in ext. Putting codex in ext duplicates it back onto the writ row (it's structurally part of WritDoc), introduces ext-vs-writ-codex divergence risk, and forces a second `clerk.setWritExt` call any time the codex changes. Putting codex on writ.codex only is the cleaner reading, matches the existing pattern, and lets `--codex` filtering use the writs book's existing codex-equivalent indexing path.

options.writ-codex-only: Codex stays on writ.codex (top-level WritDoc field). ext['cartograph'] holds only `{ stage }`. The doc projection reads `codex: writ.codex`. patchVision({ codex }) routes through clerk.edit({ id, codex }). Filtering listVisions({ codex }) uses `where: [['type', '=', 'vision'], ['codex', '=', value]]` — both clauses are top-level writ fields.
options.ext-codex-only: Codex moves into ext['cartograph'].codex; writ.codex is left unset for cartograph types. Doc projection reads from ext.cartograph.codex. patchVision({ codex }) routes through clerk.setWritExt(id, 'cartograph', { stage, codex }). Filtering listVisions({ codex }) uses `where: [['ext.cartograph.codex', '=', value]]` (json_extract — unindexed). Breaks every code path that expects writ.codex on a vision/charge/piece (writs page, codex-filter on clerk.list, codex-inheritance from parent).
options.duplicate: Codex is written to BOTH writ.codex and ext['cartograph'].codex on every createX/patchX/transitionX. Doc projection reads from ext.cartograph.codex (matches brief literal); writ.codex stays for compatibility. Adds duplication risk on every codex mutation.

decisions.rationale: Codex is structurally a top-level WritDoc field with multiple existing consumers (writs page, clerk filters, codex-inheritance). Moving it into ext re-creates the very duplication the cleanup is supposed to eliminate (writ.codex would still exist for the writ row's own purposes; ext.codex would shadow it). Keeping codex on writ.codex matches the existing pattern, avoids divergence, and lets the cartograph's listX filter route through the writs book's natural codex column. The brief's `{ stage, codex }` listing is loose shorthand for 'the projection carries stage and codex' — not a prescription about which physical slot codex lives in. The Three Defaults' 'fail-loud to silent fallback' isn't applicable here; the 'extend the API at the right layer' default actively steers toward writ.codex (the right layer for codex is the writ row, not a per-plugin ext slot).

patron.rationale: No principle speaks — confirming the primer.

decisions.question: How does listX filter at the SQL layer — read the writs book directly with a `[['type','=', T], ['ext.cartograph.stage','=', S]]` where clause, or use clerk.list({ type: T }) and post-filter in JS, or extend clerk.list to accept a generic ext filter?
decisions.context: clerk.list(filters) supports phase, type, classification, parentId — but does NOT support a generic ext.X filter. The Stacks query layer supports dot-notation field paths (json_extract on SQLite, getNestedField on memory backend) — `validateFieldName` allows the path syntax. The writs book has no `ext.cartograph.stage` index, so the SQL-layer filter would prefix-scan on the existing `type` index then post-filter via json_extract.

options.direct-readbook: listX reads the writs book directly via stacks.readBook<WritDoc>('clerk', 'writs').find({ where: [['type', '=', 'vision'], ['ext.cartograph.stage', '=', stage]?, ['codex', '=', codex]?], orderBy: ['createdAt','desc'], limit, offset }) and projects the rows. Single SQL round-trip per call.
options.clerk-list-postfilter: listX calls clerk.list({ type: 'vision', limit: large_limit }) and filters by stage in JS. Avoids depending on the json_extract path; pays a memory-shape projection. limit/offset semantics drift unless we wrap clerk.list with our own pagination — the post-filter cuts rows after the SQL limit applies.
options.extend-clerk-list: Add a generic ext filter to clerk.list (e.g. `ext: { [pluginId]: { [field]: value } }` or a flat where-merge surface). Cartograph then calls clerk.list({ type: 'vision', ext: { cartograph: { stage } } }). Larger surface change for one consumer.

decisions.rationale: Direct read on the writs book with a json_extract where clause is the natural composition: type-indexed prefix scan + json-extracted equality on stage. Pagination is correct (SQL limit/offset apply after both predicates). No extension to clerk.list needed; no large in-memory shape projection. This is also what the brief implies in §4: 'Filtering on stage becomes a JSON-field filter against writ.ext[cartograph].stage; Stacks' query layer supports this.' Three Defaults pull in the same direction: extend at the right layer (the read layer that already exists in cartograph via tx.book in transactions; same shape outside transactions via stacks.readBook).

patron.rationale: No principle speaks — confirming the primer.

decisions.question: Does the cartograph keep its `supportKit.books` declaration as `{}` (empty), drop the field entirely, or contribute new indexes elsewhere (e.g. lobby for a `[type, ext.cartograph.stage]` composite index on the writs book)?
decisions.context: Today supportKit.books contributes three book schemas with `indexes: ['stage', 'codex', 'createdAt']`. After cleanup the books vanish; the question is whether to leave `books: {}` for forward growth, drop the field entirely, or add an index contribution to the writs book. The writs book is owned by clerk; cartograph cannot directly add indexes to a book it doesn't own. The clerk's writs-book index list (`['phase', 'type', 'createdAt', 'parentId', ['phase','type'], ['phase','createdAt'], ['parentId','phase']]`) supports `[type, createdAt]` via the `[phase, createdAt]` substitute — actually no, there's no `[type, createdAt]` index. Listing visions ordered by createdAt with a type=vision filter would scan the type index then sort. For present data scale this is fine.

options.drop-books-field: Remove the supportKit.books field entirely. Cleanest — declares cartograph contributes no books.
options.empty-books: Leave supportKit.books as `{}`. Forward-compat for if the cleanup ever needs to re-introduce a cartograph-owned book. Cosmetic.
options.add-clerk-index: Coordinate with the clerk apparatus to add a `['type', 'ext.cartograph.stage']` composite index to the writs book. Requires a clerk-side change; only valuable if listX-by-stage performance becomes a concern.

decisions.rationale: Cleanest expression of intent — cartograph no longer contributes books. The 'prefer removal to deprecation' default applies. If the indexing-for-stage filter ever becomes a real bottleneck, add the composite index in a follow-on commission (the data scale today is well below the point where this matters; surveying-cascade visions are sparse).

patron.rationale: No principle speaks — confirming the primer.

decisions.question: Does the implementation export a `CARTOGRAPH_PLUGIN_ID = 'cartograph'` constant for the ext sub-slot key, or does it inline the literal string at every use site?
decisions.context: The Reckoner exports `RECKONER_PLUGIN_ID = 'reckoner' as const` (packages/plugins/reckoner/src/types.ts) and Clerk exports `CLERK_PLUGIN_ID = 'clerk'` (packages/plugins/clerk/src/types.ts). Both are used as the second arg to setWritExt/setWritStatus and as the sub-slot key on read. The cleanup adds many call sites that would address the 'cartograph' key — createX/transitionX/showX/listX/patchX × 3 types = 15 paths, plus tests. A typo in any one of those breaks the slot read silently.

options.export-constant: Add `export const CARTOGRAPH_PLUGIN_ID = 'cartograph' as const;` to types.ts and re-export from index.ts. Use it at every call site (writes, reads, tests). Mirrors RECKONER_PLUGIN_ID / CLERK_PLUGIN_ID precedent.
options.inline-literal: Inline the literal `'cartograph'` string at every call site. Smaller surface; loses the typo guard.

decisions.rationale: Matches the established convention in two precedent apparatus (Reckoner, Clerk) and gives a typo-resistant entry point that tests can also import. The cost is one constant in types.ts; the benefit is that every read/write of the ext sub-slot is name-checked at compile time.

patron.rationale: No principle speaks — confirming the primer.

decisions.question: Do the VisionDoc / ChargeDoc / PieceDoc public types keep their `[key: string]: unknown` index signature (forward-compat for future field growth) or drop it (cleaner / tighter typing)?
decisions.context: Today each doc interface has the index signature 'required to satisfy BookEntry'. After cleanup, the doc shape is no longer a BookEntry — it's a projection over WritDoc. The index signature is no longer required by any constraint. The brief §2 says 'the existing VisionDoc / ChargeDoc / PieceDoc exported types stay as projection shapes (their definitions can be retained verbatim)' — leans toward keep-verbatim. Today's `[key: string]: unknown` allows callers to pass arbitrary fields through patchX without type errors — including stage, which the cleanup-time patchX should reject (stage moves through transitionX).

options.keep-index-signature: Keep the `[key: string]: unknown` index signature on VisionDoc/ChargeDoc/PieceDoc. Definitions identical to today. Allows forward-compat field growth without breaking callers.
options.drop-index-signature: Remove the index signature. Tightens typing — patchX(id, fields) only accepts the declared optional fields. Loses forward-compat affordance.

decisions.rationale: Brief §2 says 'definitions can be retained verbatim'. Keeping the index signature preserves the surface byte-for-byte — no caller has to change anything about how they consume the shape. The forward-compat affordance survives. The patchX surface restriction (no stage) is enforced by the patch tool's Zod schema, not by the type — so dropping the index signature wouldn't actually tighten the patch boundary.

patron.rationale: No principle speaks — confirming the primer.

decisions.question: How does the cleanup-time patchX update codex on the writ row — call clerk.edit({ id, codex }) (sanctioned writ-edit path) or write directly via tx.book<WritDoc>('clerk', 'writs').patch (continues the cartograph's existing direct-write pattern)?
decisions.context: Per D2 codex stays on writ.codex. Editing writ.codex on a non-`new`-phase writ is rejected by clerk.edit (codex is one of the fields that can only be changed in `new` status). Today's patchVision (companion-doc-only) has no such restriction — the patron can change codex at any phase. clerk.edit's restriction is part of the spec/status discipline for writ-row fields. The cleanup choice is whether to inherit that restriction or sidestep it.

options.use-clerk-edit: patchX(id, { codex }) routes through clerk.edit({ id, codex }). Inherits clerk.edit's 'codex only editable in new status' rule. Means an active vision can no longer have its codex switched via patchVision — only at draft.
options.direct-tx-write: patchX writes the writs book directly via tx.book<WritDoc>('clerk', 'writs').patch(id, { codex, updatedAt: now }). Bypasses clerk.edit's status restriction (matches today's behavior — the patron can change codex at any phase).
options.clerk-edit-with-narrowed-tool: patchX uses clerk.edit; the patch tool's Zod schema is updated to only allow patching when the writ is in `new` phase (resolved by a pre-check). Loud failure on a no-longer-mutable field.

decisions.rationale: Preserves the existing patron-facing behavior (today's patchVision allows codex changes at any phase). Tightening to clerk.edit's status restriction would be a silent functionality regression — patrons who rely on switching codex on an active vision would suddenly start failing without an explicit deprecation. The Three Defaults' 'extend the API at the right layer' mildly pulls toward clerk.edit, but only if behavior parity holds; here it doesn't. If the patron wants to align with clerk.edit's stricter rule, that's a separate, explicit deprecation conversation.

patron.rationale: No principle speaks — confirming the primer.

decisions.question: When showX / listX encounter a writ of the right type (e.g. type=vision) whose ext['cartograph'].stage is missing or malformed, does the projection fail loud or fall back to a default?
decisions.context: Pre-cleanup the cartograph guarantees writ + companion doc are written together inside one transaction; reads can assume both exist. Post-cleanup the cartograph guarantees writ + ext['cartograph'].stage are written together. But a defensive check is cheap and the Three Defaults' 'fail-loud to silent fallback' applies. There are two possible failure modes: (a) the writ type is vision but ext.cartograph is absent (cartograph never wrote it — should be impossible); (b) the writ type is vision but ext.cartograph.stage is not a valid VisionStage (data corruption or a cross-version artifact).

options.fail-loud: showX/listX throw with a clear error naming the writ id and the missing field when ext.cartograph.stage is undefined or not in the per-type stage enum. Matches the framework's wider fail-loud convention (clerk.isInitial/isActive/isTerminal throw on unknown-state).
options.tolerant-fallback: showX returns a doc with stage = 'unknown' (a sentinel); listX returns the row with the sentinel. Mirrors writ-presentation's presentation-tolerance for unregistered types.
options.skip-on-list-throw-on-show: showX throws (single-item read; caller is asking for *this* doc); listX silently skips rows with malformed ext.cartograph (tolerant when many rows are involved).

decisions.rationale: The cartograph's typed API controls all writes to ext['cartograph'] — a missing or malformed sub-slot indicates either (a) a bug in the cartograph itself, or (b) someone bypassed the typed API to write a cartograph-typed writ. Both are fail-loud-worthy. The presentation-tolerance precedent (writ-presentation returning 'unknown') exists for the cross-cutting writs page that doesn't own the data; the cartograph's typed API does own the data and can be stricter. Three Defaults' 'fail-loud to silent fallback' applies directly.

patron.rationale: No principle speaks — confirming the primer.

decisions.question: Does the cleanup-time vision-apply Nth-apply 'phase unchanged but stage drifted' branch (vision-apply.ts line 408-411 — `cartograph.patchVision(boundId, { stage: sidecar.stage })`) survive verbatim, get refactored to call clerk.setWritExt directly, or get removed?
decisions.context: Today this branch handles a rare condition: the bound writ's phase already matches the sidecar's target phase, but the companion doc's stage drifted from the sidecar's stage. Pre-cleanup it patches the companion doc directly to re-couple stage with phase. Post-cleanup, the equivalent operation is to write ext.cartograph.stage to the sidecar's stage. Two ways to express it: through patchVision (which has to grow a 'stage' patch path that the patch tool refuses to expose at the CLI), or through clerk.setWritExt directly from vision-apply.

options.keep-via-patchx: patchVision({ stage }) is callable from inside the cartograph package (vision-apply uses it). The patch tool's Zod schema continues to refuse a `--stage` flag (D5/D6 in the original commission spec keep that restriction); but the typed API still allows it for internal callers like vision-apply.
options.use-setwritext-directly: vision-apply replaces the patchVision({ stage }) call with `clerk.setWritExt(boundId, CARTOGRAPH_PLUGIN_ID, { stage: sidecar.stage })`. Skips the cartograph typed API for this one rare case. Increases coupling between vision-apply and the slot-key convention.
options.drop-the-branch: Remove the branch entirely — declare that 'phase unchanged but stage drifted' is no longer a recoverable state. The patron's recourse is to also bump the writ's phase if the sidecar wants a different stage. Loses recovery path for a rare failure mode.

decisions.rationale: patchVision is the cartograph's typed API — vision-apply is part of the cartograph package and should route through it for the same reason every other call site does (validation, slot-key encapsulation, future evolvability). Allowing patchVision({ stage }) from internal callers while the patch CLI tool refuses --stage at the schema layer is exactly the layer-separation the existing design uses. The 'extend the API at the right layer' default applies — the right layer for stage drift recovery is patchX, not a direct setWritExt bypass.

patron.rationale: No principle speaks — confirming the primer.

decisions.question: Does the cartograph apparatus continue to declare `requires: ['stacks', 'clerk']` post-cleanup, or can it shed `stacks` because the only direct stacks.* call after cleanup is stacks.transaction (and the readBook for direct listX queries)?
decisions.context: Today cartograph requires both because it calls stacks.book(...) three times in start() to capture companion-book handles. After cleanup those calls go away. But createX/transitionX still need stacks.transaction for atomicity, and listX/showX still need stacks.readBook<WritDoc>('clerk', 'writs') (per D3). So stacks is still consumed. The dependency declaration drives startup ordering — keeping both ensures stacks and clerk both start before cartograph.

options.keep-both: Keep `requires: ['stacks', 'clerk']`. No change to the apparatus startup wiring.
options.drop-stacks: Drop to `requires: ['clerk']`. Risk: cartograph might be started before stacks, breaking stacks.transaction / stacks.readBook calls.
options.implicit-via-clerk: Drop to `requires: ['clerk']`. Reasoning: clerk requires stacks transitively, so stacks is started by the time clerk is ready. Fragile — the contract isn't a direct dep.

decisions.rationale: Cartograph still consumes stacks directly (transaction, readBook). Dropping the explicit dep relies on transitive ordering which isn't a contract. Keep `requires: ['stacks', 'clerk']` verbatim. The 'extend the API at the right layer' default mildly applies — the right layer to declare a dep is the apparatus that actually consumes the API.

patron.rationale: No principle speaks — confirming the primer.

decisions.question: When createX reads a parent writ (charge under a vision; piece under a charge or piece) inside its transaction for parent validation, does it continue to use the existing tx.book<WritDoc>('clerk', 'writs').get pattern, or refactor to call clerk.show / clerk.list under the transaction?
decisions.context: Today's validateParent helper (cartograph.ts lines 219-246) uses `txWritsBook.get(parentId)` directly. After cleanup if D1 picks the hybrid pattern, createX still operates inside a stacks.transaction (because the writ put + ext stamp need atomicity if D1 routes through the writs book, or because clerk.post + setWritExt need a shared transaction either way). Either tx.book.get or clerk.show works inside the transaction (clerk.show opens its own which flattens). Direct tx.book.get is one less round-trip.

options.keep-direct-get: Continue using tx.book<WritDoc>('clerk', 'writs').get(parentId) inside the transaction. One read; no clerk-method call.
options.use-clerk-show: Call clerk.show(parentId) inside the transaction. Consistent with clerk being the cartograph's other dep; trivially more 'API-layer' cleanly. Same number of reads (clerk.show wraps writs.get with a not-found error).

decisions.rationale: Parent validation is happening inside an existing tx context where `tx.book` is the natural handle. Switching to clerk.show is cosmetic — same code paths, same round-trip count, slightly less direct. Keep the existing pattern.

patron.rationale: No principle speaks — confirming the primer.

decisions.question: Should the surveying-cascade doc updates explicitly enumerate the SurveyDoc-shape rewrite per the brief's bullet list (drop targetNodeId/rigName/completedAt; rigVersion + surveyorId move to status['surveyor']), or paraphrase the brief's intent and leave the precise field-set decisions to Commission C?
decisions.context: The brief §6 names §3.4's rewrite explicitly: 'Rewrite this section so the survey-writ envelope metadata (rigVersion, surveyorId, completedAt — minus targetNodeId, which is already writ.parentId, and minus rigName, which is already writ.type) lives in status['surveyor'] / ext['surveyor'] sub-slots on the survey writ rather than in a books.surveys companion book. Note that completedAt is redundant with writ.resolvedAt. The substrate is the only writer.' The doc is a settled architectural reference; this commission is updating it to pre-design Commission C's substrate. Either we land the prescribed shape verbatim (Commission C inherits) or we paraphrase and let Commission C decide the final field set.

options.land-prescribed-shape: Rewrite §3.4 to declare the post-cleanup SurveyDoc shape exactly as the brief enumerates: status['surveyor'] = { rigVersion, surveyorId } and (implicit) writ.resolvedAt for completion timestamp. Drop targetNodeId / rigName / completedAt as redundant. Substrate is the only writer.
options.paraphrase-defer-to-c: Rewrite §3.4 to declare 'envelope metadata moves to status['surveyor'] / ext['surveyor']; the precise field set is settled in Commission C', without enumerating the fields. Slight loss of design lock-in but Commission C gets fresh latitude.
options.land-prescribed-with-followup-marker: Land the prescribed shape verbatim, plus a one-line note 'Commission C may refine this field set as substrate concerns crystallize.' Splits the difference.

decisions.rationale: The brief §6 is explicit about the field-set rewrite — pre-emption applies. Commission C's job becomes 'build the substrate against this shape'; landing the shape now means Commission C inherits a settled architectural artifact rather than re-litigating it. The brief is the patron's voice at planning time; following it directly is the right move.

patron.rationale: No principle speaks — confirming the primer.

decisions.question: Does the surveying-cascade §3.6 doc rewrite spell out the specific stacks.watch invocation pattern (stacks.watch<WritDoc>('clerk', 'writs', handler, { failOnError: false }) plus a handler-side filter on entry.type) or just describe the conceptual shift from per-book to writ-type-filtered subscription?
decisions.context: Today §3.6 enumerates three book-event streams (`book.cartograph.visions.{created,updated}`, etc.). The replacement is conceptually 'one subscription, type filter in the handler'. Stacks supports stacks.watch on a single (ownerId, bookName) pair; there's no built-in type-side filter argument. The substrate's handler does its own `if (entry.type !== 'vision' && entry.type !== 'charge' && entry.type !== 'piece') return;` filter.

options.include-call-shape: Rewrite §3.6 with the concrete stacks.watch invocation and the handler-side filter pattern. Reads as 'how to' substrate-implementation guidance.
options.describe-conceptual-only: Rewrite §3.6 describing the shift conceptually ('one filtered subscription on the writs book') without specifying the call shape. Leaves substrate-implementation choices to Commission C.
options.split-the-difference: Rewrite §3.6 with the conceptual shift in prose plus a small code block illustrating the call shape. Settled architectural reference + an implementer hint.

decisions.rationale: §3.6 is a settled architectural reference describing how the cascade fires. The conceptual shift (one subscription + handler filter) is the load-bearing change; a small code block makes it concrete enough that Commission C doesn't have to derive the call shape. The doc convention elsewhere in the file (e.g. §3.4 already shows interface SurveyDoc; §3.9 shows interface CreateChargeArgs) leans this way — illustrate + prose.

patron.rationale: No principle speaks — confirming the primer.

decisions.question: What happens to historical `cartograph/{visions,charges,pieces}` rows that exist in any guild's persisted Stacks data when the cleanup ships — automatic migration, manual cleanup script, or simply orphaned?
decisions.context: Brief §5 says 'There is no migration of historical companion-doc rows — the cartograph is recently-shipped and nothing in production-style guild data depends on those books.' The books simply vanish from the apparatus's supportKit; their tables remain in any persisted SQLite database but are never read or written again. The ensureBook scaffolding for them is not invoked, so a fresh guild boot doesn't recreate them. An old guild boot finds the tables intact but unreferenced.

options.no-migration-orphan: Honor the brief: drop the books from supportKit, don't touch existing data. The orphan tables sit harmlessly in old SQLite files. Cleanest expression; matches the brief verbatim.
options.drop-tables-on-boot: Add a one-time startup-window step that drops the three tables if they exist. Cleans up the orphan data; adds a small permanent boot-time check.
options.ship-cleanup-script: Add a separate one-shot script (`nsg cartograph migrate-from-companion-books`) that an operator runs once to clean up. Manual; no boot-time overhead; rarely-needed.

decisions.rationale: Brief §5 pre-empts: 'no migration of historical companion-doc rows'. The cartograph is recently-shipped; orphan tables in any SQLite file are inert and harmless. Dropping tables on boot adds a permanent startup cost for a one-time concern. A separate migration script is an over-engineered solution for data that the brief explicitly says no one depends on.

patron.rationale: No principle speaks — confirming the primer.

decisions.question: When the cleanup-time createX is called with a structurally-typed caller that smuggles `ext` through the request (CreateVisionRequest doesn't expose `ext`, but a (request as { ext?: unknown }).ext path could pass one), does the cartograph reject loudly, silently strip, or silently forward?
decisions.context: createVision currently has a defensive guard for the parentId smuggling case (cartograph.ts lines 257-264 throws on a non-empty parentId even though CreateVisionRequest doesn't expose it). The pattern is: type-safe at the schema boundary; defensive at runtime. After cleanup, ext is similarly off-limits (cartograph owns ext['cartograph']; another plugin's ext sub-slot would land via setWritExt anyway and is irrelevant to the create call).

options.reject-loud: Mirror the parentId guard pattern: if request.ext is set, throw with a clear error naming the field. Defensive programming; matches existing style.
options.silent-strip: Strip request.ext silently before the writ put. Lower friction; loses fail-loud signal.
options.silent-forward: Forward request.ext if present (would clobber the cartograph's own ext sub-slot if ext.cartograph happened to be set). Dangerous; loses slot-write convention.

decisions.rationale: Mirrors the existing parentId guard pattern. Three Defaults' 'fail-loud to silent fallback' applies. The cost is a single defensive check on a code path that should never fire in practice; the value is that any structural-type bypass is caught at the boundary.

patron.rationale: No principle speaks — confirming the primer.

spec:
# Cartograph: collapse companion docs into `writ.ext['cartograph']`

## Intent

Eliminate the three Stacks companion books (`cartograph/{visions,charges,pieces}`) that the cartograph apparatus currently maintains alongside each writ row, and move the typed-metadata payload (`stage`) into the sanctioned `writ.ext['cartograph']` sub-slot on the writ itself. The cartograph's typed API surface — 15 methods across `vision/charge/piece` × `create/transition/show/list/patch` — stays byte-for-byte stable; only the storage shape underneath changes. The surveying-cascade architecture doc is rewritten in lockstep so the future surveyor substrate (Commission C) is briefed against the post-cleanup shape from day one.

## Rationale

The current two-write boundary (writ row + companion doc, written together inside one `stacks.transaction`) was a recently-shipped "atomic typed surface" experiment. With `WritDoc.ext[pluginId]` available as the sanctioned per-plugin slot and `setWritExt` providing transactional sub-slot rmw, the companion books are now a duplication: every consumer reads the writ row anyway, the books carry only `stage` plus a duplicated `codex` field, no external code consumes them, and the surveying-cascade substrate planned for Commission C wants a single CDC subscription on the writs book filtered by type rather than three per-book subscriptions. Collapsing the storage simplifies the apparatus, removes one layer of duplicated lifecycle/parent validation in `transitionX`, and pre-aligns the substrate's CDC observer shape.

## Scope & Blast Radius

The work is **internal to `@shardworks/cartograph-apparatus`** plus a focused rewrite of `docs/architecture/surveying-cascade.md`. There are no external code consumers — a repo-wide search for `VisionDoc`, `ChargeDoc`, `PieceDoc`, `CartographApi`, or the three `cartograph/{visions,charges,pieces}` book names finds matches only inside the cartograph package itself (source + tests + README) and in the surveying-cascade arch doc.

Concerns the implementer must verify, not just trust the brief on:

- **Companion-book references everywhere they appear.** The three book names (`cartograph/visions`, `cartograph/charges`, `cartograph/pieces`) appear in: the apparatus `start()` block, `supportKit.books`, every `createX`/`transitionX` body, the apparatus integration test fixture, the tools-CLI integration test fixture, the vision-apply integration test fixture (including a CDC `stacks.watch` call), the cartograph README's "Companion documents" subsection / "Books" table / header bullet, the cartograph and types module-level docstrings, the render-tool docstring, and the vision-apply tool docstring. Verify with `grep -rn "cartograph.*\(visions\|charges\|pieces\)" packages/plugins/cartograph/ docs/` after the changes — no live references should remain (the surveying-cascade doc may keep historical context lines but its load-bearing prose must use the new shape).
- **CDC-event tests on the writs book.** Three test files watch the cartograph companion books today (`stacks.watch('cartograph', 'visions', …)` etc.). After cleanup these must watch `clerk` `writs` filtered by `entry.type` in the handler. The single-event-per-apply guarantee is preserved by the apparatus's transaction discipline — the tests must continue to assert it.
- **The surveying-cascade arch doc.** §3.4, §3.6, and §3.7 are explicitly named for rewrite. Additionally, the implementer must grep the doc for `book\.cartograph` references in §2's flow diagram and §4.1's worked example and reconcile them for consistency.

There are **no migrations**. Per the locked decision (D14), historical companion-book rows in any persisted SQLite database are simply orphaned — the books vanish from `supportKit`, are never read or written again, and any leftover tables sit harmlessly. There is no boot-time cleanup, no migration script, no startup check.

The cartograph package's runtime dependencies stay verbatim at `requires: ['stacks', 'clerk']` — `stacks.transaction` and `stacks.readBook` are still consumed directly.

## Decisions

| #   | Decision | Default | Rationale |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| D1  | How `createX` / `transitionX` achieve atomic writ + ext writes | **hybrid**: `createX` keeps the existing direct `tx.book<WritDoc>('clerk','writs').put` pattern with `ext: { cartograph: { stage } }` populated inline on the put (one Phase 1 CDC create event); `transitionX` switches to `clerk.transition` + `clerk.setWritExt` under one shared `stacks.transaction` (removes the heaviest duplicated lifecycle validation). | One CDC event per logical create; lifecycle-validation duplication eliminated for transitions. Phase-1 update-then-update on transition is a non-issue because cartograph types declare no `childrenBehavior` cascade engagement, and the only Phase 1 writs-book observer skips phase-unchanged updates. |
| D2  | Where codex lives on a cartograph writ | **`writ.codex` only** (top-level WritDoc field). `ext['cartograph']` holds only `{ stage }`. | Codex is structurally a top-level WritDoc concern (writs page, clerk's `--codex` filter, codex-inheritance from parent). Putting it in ext would re-create the very duplication this cleanup eliminates. The brief's `{ stage, codex }` listing is loose shorthand for the projection shape, not a storage prescription. |
| D3  | How `listX` filters at the SQL layer | **direct readbook**: `listX` calls `stacks.readBook<WritDoc>('clerk','writs').find({ where: [['type','=','vision'], …] })` directly and projects the rows. Stage filter uses the dot-notation field path `'ext.cartograph.stage'` (json_extract on SQLite, `getNestedField` on memory). | Single SQL round-trip; pagination is correct (limit/offset apply after both predicates); no extension to `clerk.list` needed; the natural composition of type-indexed prefix scan + json-extracted equality. |
| D4  | What happens to `supportKit.books` | **drop the field entirely**. Remove the `books:` block from `supportKit`. | Cleanest expression of intent — cartograph contributes no books. If indexing-for-stage ever becomes a real bottleneck, add a `[type, ext.cartograph.stage]` composite index on the clerk-owned writs book in a follow-on commission. |
| D5  | Plugin-id constant for the ext sub-slot key | **export `CARTOGRAPH_PLUGIN_ID = 'cartograph' as const`** from `types.ts` and re-export from `index.ts`. Use it at every read/write of the ext sub-slot, including in tests. | Mirrors the precedent in Reckoner (`RECKONER_PLUGIN_ID`) and Clerk (`CLERK_PLUGIN_ID`); typo-resistant entry point that tests can also import. |
| D6  | `VisionDoc` / `ChargeDoc` / `PieceDoc` index signature | **keep `[key: string]: unknown`**. Public type definitions stay verbatim. | Brief intent is that the projection types stay byte-for-byte stable. The patchX surface restriction (no stage) is enforced at the patch tool's Zod schema, not by the type's index signature. |
| D7  | How `patchX` updates `writ.codex` | **direct tx-book write**: `patchX` writes the writs book directly via `tx.book<WritDoc>('clerk','writs').patch(id, { codex, updatedAt: now })`, bypassing `clerk.edit`'s "codex only editable in `new` status" rule. | Preserves today's patron-facing behavior (codex changes allowed at any phase). Routing through `clerk.edit` would be a silent functionality regression for any patron relying on switching codex on an active vision. |
| D8  | Behavior when `showX` / `listX` encounter a typed writ whose `ext['cartograph'].stage` is missing or not a valid stage enum | **fail loud**. Throw with a clear error naming the writ id and the missing/malformed field. | The cartograph's typed API owns all writes to `ext['cartograph']`. Missing or malformed sub-slot indicates either a cartograph bug or a bypass — both are fail-loud-worthy. The presentation-tolerance precedent is for cross-cutting consumers that don't own the data. |
| D9  | The Nth-apply "phase unchanged but stage drifted" branch in vision-apply | **keep via patchVision**. `vision-apply` continues to call `cartograph.patchVision(boundId, { stage: sidecar.stage })`. The patch CLI tool's Zod schema continues to refuse `--stage`; the typed API still accepts it for internal callers like vision-apply. | Layer separation — the right layer for stage-drift recovery is `patchX`. Switching to a direct `clerk.setWritExt` call from vision-apply would couple it to the slot-key convention and bypass the typed API for a single rare case. |
| D10 | Apparatus `requires:` declaration | **keep both**: `requires: ['stacks', 'clerk']`. | Cartograph still consumes `stacks.transaction` and `stacks.readBook` directly. The dep declaration drives startup ordering; transitive ordering through clerk isn't a contract. |
| D11 | Parent validation reads inside the transaction | **keep direct `tx.book.get`**. `validateParent` continues to use `tx.book<WritDoc>('clerk','writs').get(parentId)`. | Cosmetic alternative; same code paths and round-trip count. The existing pattern is the natural handle inside an active tx. |
| D12 | Surveying-cascade §3.4 SurveyDoc rewrite scope | **land prescribed shape verbatim**. Drop `targetNodeId` (redundant with `writ.parentId`), `rigName` (redundant with `writ.type`), `completedAt` (redundant with `writ.resolvedAt`). Move `rigVersion` and `surveyorId` to `status['surveyor']`. The substrate is the only writer. | Brief is explicit about the field-set rewrite; pre-empting now means Commission C inherits a settled architectural artifact rather than re-litigating it. |
| D13 | Surveying-cascade §3.6 doc rewrite shape | **split the difference**: conceptual prose describing the shift from three per-book streams to one filtered subscription on the writs book, plus a small code block illustrating the call shape (`stacks.watch<WritDoc>('clerk','writs', handler, { failOnError: false })` with handler-side `entry.type` filter). | Matches the doc's existing convention (interface excerpts in §3.4 / §3.9). Concrete enough that Commission C doesn't have to derive the call shape; conceptual enough that substrate-implementation choices stay open. |
| D14 | Historical `cartograph/{visions,charges,pieces}` rows in persisted Stacks data | **no migration — orphan**. The books vanish from supportKit; existing tables in any SQLite database sit harmlessly unreferenced. | Brief pre-empts: no migration of historical companion-doc rows. The cartograph is recently-shipped; orphan tables are inert. Boot-time cleanup or a migration script would be over-engineered. |
| D15 | `createX` defensive handling of structurally-typed callers smuggling `ext` through the request | **reject loud**. Mirror the existing `parentId` guard: if `(request as { ext?: unknown }).ext` is present, throw with a clear error naming the field. | Mirrors the existing `parentId` guard pattern in `createVision`. The cost is one defensive check on a code path that should never fire in practice; the value is that any structural-type bypass is caught at the boundary. |

## Acceptance Signal

1. **`pnpm -w test` passes** with the cartograph package's three integration test files (`cartograph.test.ts`, `tools.test.ts`, `tools/vision-apply.test.ts`) updated to: (a) drop the `cartograph/{visions,charges,pieces}` `ensureBook` calls in their fixtures; (b) move CDC-event watches from the cartograph companion books to `clerk` `writs` filtered by `entry.type`; (c) preserve the single-event-per-apply assertion under the new shape.
2. **`pnpm -w typecheck` passes.** No callers of `cartograph.{create,transition,show,list,patch}{Vision,Charge,Piece}` need source changes — the API surface is byte-for-byte stable.
3. **No live references to the cartograph companion books remain.** A grep for `cartograph.*\(visions\|charges\|pieces\)` across `packages/plugins/cartograph/src/` and `docs/` returns no matches in active code paths or in the load-bearing prose of `surveying-cascade.md` (historical/contextual mentions in narrative are acceptable; `supportKit.books`, `tx.book(...)`, and `stacks.watch(...)` calls referencing those books must be gone).
4. **`nsg vision create … && nsg vision show <id>` round-trip works**, with the show output's projected `VisionDoc` shape (and the analogous shapes for charge/piece) matching today's byte-for-byte JSON output. The same verification holds for `nsg vision list`, `nsg vision transition`, `nsg vision patch`, and `nsg vision apply`. The lifecycle-aware `Stage:` row in show/list rendering is unchanged.
5. **`nsg writ show <vision-id>` surfaces `ext: { cartograph: { stage: '<stage>' } }`** in the raw JSON projection, consistent with how `ext.reckoner` and `ext.surveyor` already render today.
6. **`docs/architecture/surveying-cascade.md` §3.4, §3.6, and §3.7 are rewritten** to the post-cleanup shape per D12 / D13: §3.4 declares `status['surveyor'] = { rigVersion, surveyorId }` with `writ.parentId` / `writ.type` / `writ.resolvedAt` carrying the dropped envelope fields; §3.6 describes the single filtered writs-book subscription with a small `stacks.watch` code block; §3.7's substrate plugin shape drops `books.surveys` from the "Owns" list and replaces "Stamps SurveyDoc on completion" with "Stamps `status['surveyor']` on completion." Incidental `book.cartograph` references in §2 / §4.1 are reconciled.
7. **`CARTOGRAPH_PLUGIN_ID` is exported** from `packages/plugins/cartograph/src/types.ts`, re-exported from `index.ts`, and used at every read/write site for the ext sub-slot inside the cartograph package and its tests.
8. **`createX` defensive `ext`-smuggling guard fires.** A direct call constructed as `cartograph.createVision({ ...validRequest, ext: {} } as any)` throws with a clear error naming the offending field, mirroring the existing `parentId` guard.

## Reference Material

### `WritDoc.ext` slot contract

Source: `packages/plugins/clerk/src/types.ts` (lines 87–174 — the `WritDoc` interface, with the `ext?: Record<string, unknown>` field and its slot-write contract docstring). Role: defines the sanctioned per-plugin slot the cartograph is moving its payload into.

The contract:

> There is exactly one sanctioned slot-write path: `ClerkApi.setWritExt(writId, pluginId, value)`, which performs a transactional read-modify-write on the sub-slot keyed by `pluginId` so sibling sub-slots are preserved under concurrent writers.
>
> `transition()` silently strips `ext` from its body, and the generic `put()` / `patch()` paths on the writs book are not supported slot-write mechanisms — every route other than `setWritExt()` would wholesale-replace the slot and clobber sibling sub-slots.

This contract governs `transitionX` (which routes through `clerk.transition` + `clerk.setWritExt` per D1 to honor the sanctioned path). It does *not* govern `createX` — `createX` writes the writ row for the first time via direct `tx.book<WritDoc>('clerk','writs').put`, where the ext slot is being initialized (not modified), and `setWritExt` is only mandatory for read-modify-write of an existing ext slot. Inlining `ext: { [CARTOGRAPH_PLUGIN_ID]: { stage } }` on the initial put is the correct shape.

### `setWritExt` impl behavior

Source: `packages/plugins/clerk/src/clerk.ts` (lines 1188–1213). Role: the API the `transitionX` rewrite calls into.

```typescript
async setWritExt(writId, pluginId, value): Promise<WritDoc> {
  if (!writId) throw new Error('setWritExt: writId is required.');
  if (!pluginId) throw new Error('setWritExt: pluginId is required.');
  return stacks.transaction(async (tx) => {
    const txWrits = tx.book<WritDoc>('clerk', 'writs');
    const existing = await txWrits.get(writId);
    if (!existing) throw new Error(`Writ "${writId}" not found.`);
    const prevExt = (existing.ext ?? {}) as Record<string, unknown>;
    const nextExt: Record<string, unknown> = { ...prevExt, [pluginId]: value };
    return txWrits.patch(writId, {
      ext: nextExt,
      updatedAt: new Date().toISOString(),
    });
  });
},
```

Key behaviors the implementer must lean on: it opens its own `stacks.transaction` which **flattens** when invoked inside an outer transaction (see `packages/plugins/stacks/src/stacks-core.ts` line 192 — "If already in a transaction, just run (no nesting — flattened)"); preserves sibling sub-slots; bumps `updatedAt`; emits a CDC update event on the writs row. So in `transitionX`, wrapping `clerk.transition(id, to, fields)` followed by `clerk.setWritExt(id, CARTOGRAPH_PLUGIN_ID, { stage })` inside one `stacks.transaction(...)` produces a flattened single transaction, two Phase 1 update events that coalesce to one Phase 2 event.

### Stacks where-clause for ext-field filtering

Sources: `packages/plugins/stacks/src/query.ts` lines 14–21 (`SAFE_FIELD_RE = /^[A-Za-z0-9_.-]+$/` — permits dot-notation paths), `packages/plugins/stacks/src/sqlite-backend.ts` lines 47–53 (`toJsonPath` / `jsonExtract` translate `'ext.cartograph.stage'` → `json_extract(content, '$.ext.cartograph.stage')`), and `packages/plugins/stacks/src/field-utils.ts` lines 14–22 (`getNestedField` walks dot-paths in JS for the memory backend). Role: confirms that the field path the `listX` rewrite uses is supported on both backends without indexing changes.

Both backends accept `where: [['ext.cartograph.stage', '=', value]]`. SQLite executes it as `json_extract(...)`; memory uses `getNestedField`. No clerk-side index contribution is required.

### Plugin-id constant convention

Source: `packages/plugins/reckoner/src/types.ts` and `packages/plugins/clerk/src/types.ts`. Role: existing precedent for the `CARTOGRAPH_PLUGIN_ID` export (D5).

```typescript
export const RECKONER_PLUGIN_ID = 'reckoner' as const;   // packages/plugins/reckoner/src/types.ts
export const CLERK_PLUGIN_ID = 'clerk';                  // packages/plugins/clerk/src/types.ts
```

Apply the same shape: `export const CARTOGRAPH_PLUGIN_ID = 'cartograph' as const;` in `packages/plugins/cartograph/src/types.ts`, re-exported from `packages/plugins/cartograph/src/index.ts`.

### CDC coalescing inside a transaction

Source: `packages/plugins/stacks/src/cdc.ts` lines 87–145 (`coalesceEvents`). Role: explains why the D1 hybrid is correct — Phase 2 sees one coalesced event regardless of the in-tx event count.

> In-transaction events on the same row coalesce. `create`-then-`update` becomes a single Phase 2 `create` event with the final state. So `clerk.post` + `clerk.setWritExt` under one tx emits one Phase 2 create event. Phase 1 still sees both (failOnError handlers run synchronously inside the tx).

For cartograph types specifically: no Phase 1 observer cares (children-behavior-engine is the only Phase 1 writs-book observer; it skips phase-unchanged updates and cartograph types declare no `childrenBehavior` block). Phase 2 observers (sentinel, clockworks lifecycle observer) see one coalesced event per logical operation regardless of the D1 sub-pattern.

### Surveying-cascade arch doc — current §3.4 SurveyDoc shape

Source: `docs/architecture/surveying-cascade.md` lines 175–190. Role: identifies the exact section §3.4 the rewrite replaces (per D12).

```typescript
interface SurveyDoc {
  id: string;             // primary key — the survey writ id
  targetNodeId: string;   // the cartograph node being surveyed
  rigName: string;
  rigVersion: string;
  surveyorId: string;
  completedAt: string;
}
```

The replacement (per D12): drop `targetNodeId` (redundant with `writ.parentId`), `rigName` (redundant with `writ.type`), `completedAt` (redundant with `writ.resolvedAt`). Move `rigVersion` and `surveyorId` to `status['surveyor']`. The substrate is the only writer.

### Surveying-cascade arch doc — current §3.6 / §3.7 shape

Source: `docs/architecture/surveying-cascade.md` lines 210–238 (§3.6) and 240–266 (§3.7). Role: identifies the exact sections the rewrite replaces (per D12 / D13).

§3.6 currently enumerates three book-event streams: `book.cartograph.visions.{created,updated}`, `book.cartograph.charges.{created,updated}`, `book.cartograph.pieces.{created,updated}`. The rewrite (per D13, "split the difference") replaces this with prose describing the single filtered subscription on the writs book, plus a small code block illustrating the call shape:

```typescript
stacks.watch<WritDoc>('clerk', 'writs', (entry) => {
  if (entry.type !== 'vision' && entry.type !== 'charge' && entry.type !== 'piece') return;
  // … substrate handler logic …
}, { failOnError: false });
```

The single-event-per-apply guarantee discussion stays — its phrasing shifts from "wrap createVision + transition in one transaction" to "the cartograph's createX/transitionX primitives are already transactional" with the same outcome (one CDC-significant phase transition per apply).

§3.7's substrate plugin shape bullet list under `@shardworks/surveyor-apparatus`: drop `books.surveys` from the "Owns" list; substrate now owns the `status['surveyor']` and `ext['surveyor']` slots on survey writs. Replace "Stamps SurveyDoc on completion" with "Stamps `status['surveyor']` on completion."

### Cartograph apparatus — module-level docstring

Source: `packages/plugins/cartograph/src/cartograph.ts` lines 1–34. Role: identifies a docstring that explicitly describes the "three companion books under owner id `cartograph`" pattern and must be rewritten to describe the post-cleanup shape (single writ row, `ext['cartograph']` sub-slot).

### Cartograph types module — public type definitions and module docstring

Source: `packages/plugins/cartograph/src/types.ts`. Role: the module docstring (lines 1–17) and per-interface docstrings reference "companion document … keyed by the writ id one-for-one" and "vision text lives on `writ.body`; the companion docs carry typed metadata only." These need rewriting to describe the projection-over-writ-row shape. The `VisionStage` / `ChargeStage` / `PieceStage` enums (lines 36, 48, 60), the `VisionFilters` / `ChargeFilters` / `PieceFilters` shapes (lines 133–166), the `Create*Request` / `Transition*Request` shapes (lines 187–282), and the `CartographApi` interface (lines 311–350) all stay verbatim per the brief.

### Today's `createVision` body

Source: `packages/plugins/cartograph/src/cartograph.ts` lines 308–338. Role: the existing pattern the D1-`createX`-direct-tx-write rewrite mirrors.

```typescript
return stacks.transaction(async (tx) => {
  const txWritsBook = tx.book<WritDoc>('clerk', 'writs');
  const txVisionsBook = tx.book<VisionDoc>('cartograph', 'visions');
  const childId = generateId('w', 6);
  const now = new Date().toISOString();
  const writ: WritDoc = {
    id: childId, type: 'vision', phase: requestedPhase,
    title: request.title, body: request.body,
    ...(request.codex !== undefined ? { codex: request.codex } : {}),
    createdAt: now, updatedAt: now,
  };
  await txWritsBook.put(writ);
  const doc: VisionDoc = {
    id: childId, stage: requestedStage,
    ...(request.codex !== undefined ? { codex: request.codex } : {}),
    createdAt: now, updatedAt: now,
  };
  await txVisionsBook.put(doc);
  return doc;
});
```

Post-cleanup: the writ put carries `ext: { [CARTOGRAPH_PLUGIN_ID]: { stage: requestedStage } }`; the companion-book put disappears entirely; the return value is a projection over the persisted writ row.

### Today's `transitionVision` body

Source: `packages/plugins/cartograph/src/cartograph.ts` lines 360–417. Role: the existing pattern the D1-`transitionX`-clerk-call rewrite replaces.

```typescript
return stacks.transaction(async (tx) => {
  const txWritsBook = tx.book<WritDoc>('clerk', 'writs');
  const txVisionsBook = tx.book<VisionDoc>('cartograph', 'visions');
  const writ = await txWritsBook.get(id);
  // … duplicates clerk.transition's allowedTransitions / classification checks …
  const writPatch: Partial<Omit<WritDoc, 'id'>> = {
    phase: request.phase, updatedAt: now,
    ...(isTerminal ? { resolvedAt: now } : {}),
    ...(request.resolution !== undefined ? { resolution: request.resolution } : {}),
  };
  await txWritsBook.patch(id, writPatch);
  return txVisionsBook.patch(id, { stage: request.stage, updatedAt: now });
});
```

Post-cleanup: the body becomes `stacks.transaction(async () => { await clerk.transition(id, request.phase, request.resolution !== undefined ? { resolution: request.resolution } : undefined); await clerk.setWritExt(id, CARTOGRAPH_PLUGIN_ID, { stage: request.stage }); return projectVisionDoc(await stacks.readBook<WritDoc>('clerk','writs').get(id)); })` — call shape is illustrative only; the implementer chooses how to thread the projection through. The duplicated lifecycle/classification validation block (today's lines 304–307 comment "the cost of being a typed atomic surface") goes away.

### Reckoner ext-slot precedent

Source: `packages/plugins/reckoner/src/reckoner.ts` line 1269 (the `clerk.setWritExt(writId, RECKONER_PLUGIN_ID, ext)` call site). Role: precedent for the slot-key convention. **Do not Read** for the *atomicity* shape — Reckoner's `petition()` is two-step and non-atomic by design ("D7" in its own docs); the cartograph's create+stamp is atomic by design (D1 hybrid). The relevant comparison is the slot ownership / constant convention only.

### CDC observer landscape (constraint-only)

Source: `packages/plugins/clerk/src/children-behavior-engine.ts`. Role: load-bearing constraint analysis only. **Do not Read.** — the relevant facts are: the engine fires only on phase-changes (`entry.phase !== prev.phase`); a `setWritExt` update with phase unchanged is a silent no-op for the cascade; cartograph types declare no `childrenBehavior` block, so even phase-changes don't engage the cascade for vision/charge/piece. This is why the D1 hybrid's two-Phase-1-events-on-transition is a non-issue.

### Cartograph package files cited as pointers (no content excerpts here)

These files are part of the cleanup's blast radius but the implementer does not need additional reference material from them beyond the inventory's location data:

- `packages/plugins/cartograph/src/cartograph.test.ts` — apparatus integration tests (drop `ensureBook` calls in `buildFixture`; rewire CDC-event test from cartograph-visions watch to writs-book watch with type filter; delete the "declares the three companion books with the expected indexes" test; rewrite the companion-book-separation tests against the new internals; preserve the lifecycle-coupling tests).
- `packages/plugins/cartograph/src/tools.test.ts` — tools-CLI integration tests (drop `ensureBook` calls in `buildFixture`; remainder unaffected).
- `packages/plugins/cartograph/src/tools/vision-apply.test.ts` — vision-apply integration tests (drop `ensureBook` calls in `buildFixture`; move the CDC-counter watch from `cartograph/visions` to `clerk/writs` filtered by type; preserve first-apply / Nth-apply / error-path / surveyor-payload assertions).
- `packages/plugins/cartograph/src/tools/render.ts` — `composeShow` / `projectWrit` rendering helpers; no public-shape changes (the JSON shape per the original commission's D8 stays).
- `packages/plugins/cartograph/src/tools/vision-apply.ts` — vision-apply CLI; first-apply and Nth-apply paths unchanged in argument shape; the Nth-apply stage-drift branch continues to call `cartograph.patchVision(boundId, { stage })` per D9.

## What NOT To Do
